topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash. Mark stared at the attendance sheet, the ink blurring slightly under the harsh fluorescent lights of the school office. His jaw tightened, a familiar knot forming just beneath his skin. He’d been trying to shake off the lingering exhaustion of a week spent wrestling with an impossible spreadsheet, and now this. A name on the roster, a face that swam before his eyes—Ms. Eleanor Vance. The teacher of his son, Leo. A slow, heavy deflation settled in his chest. He rubbed his temples, the motion stiff and reluctant. It felt like a deliberate, unwelcome intrusion, a misplaced piece in an otherwise orderly life. He didn't bother to call the school; the thought of facing that familiar, well-meaning face again felt like an unnecessary imposition on his already frayed nerves.

[story 2]

Sarah slammed the car door shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet driveway. She slumped against the steering wheel, letting out a long, ragged sigh that seemed to empty her lungs of all remaining patience. The discovery had hit her like a physical blow: Leo, her bright, chaotic boy, sharing a classroom with the woman who had shaped his early years. A sourness curdled in her stomach, making her hands clench into fists on the steering wheel. She glanced at the rearview mirror, seeing only her own tired reflection, etched with a deep weariness. Why did the universe insist on these unexpected alignments? She gripped the wheel tighter, her knuckles white, wishing the pavement would simply swallow her whole.

[story 3]

David paced the length of his living room, his movements jerky and sharp. He stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent suburban street. The realization had settled over him, cold and heavy, like a damp blanket. Ms. Vance. The name surfaced repeatedly, each mention bringing a tightening sensation to his throat. He picked up a remote control and tossed it onto the sofa cushion with unnecessary force. His voice, when he finally spoke, was low and edged with irritation. "This is just… inconvenient," he muttered to the empty room, the words tasting bitter. He walked over to the bookshelf, running a hand over the spines of the books, seeking some anchor in the sudden, unsettling shift of reality.
